title: Decorative concrete cost: acid stain vs dye vs overlay
excerpt: Decorative concrete costs $2-$15 per square foot in 2026 — acid stain $2-$6, dye $3-$8, microtopping overlay $4-$10, stamped overlay $7-$15.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed decorative-concrete, slab, driveway, and patio prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, decorative concrete pricing spans $2-$15 per square foot installed depending on method: acid stain $2-$6, water-based dye $3-$8, polished concrete $4-$8, microtopping overlay $4-$10, and stamped overlay $7-$15. Each method has a different appearance, durability profile, and substrate requirement — acid stain etches the surface to produce variegated mineral colors that no two slabs share, while dye produces uniform tone and overlay methods build a new surface on top of cured concrete. The four methods together cover the most common decorative-concrete jobs on existing residential slabs: refreshing a garage floor, transforming an unfinished basement, dressing up an exterior patio, or rescuing a worn-out driveway.

faqs.question: How much does acid stain cost on concrete in 2026?
faqs.answer: Acid stain runs $2-$6 per square foot installed in 2026, with $3-$4 per square foot typical for a single-color application with sealer on a clean prepared slab. On a 400-square-foot patio, that's $800-$2,400 total — typically $1,200-$1,600. Acid stain etches the concrete surface to produce variegated mineral colors; no two slabs ever look identical.

faqs.question: Is concrete dye better than acid stain?
faqs.answer: Concrete dye delivers more uniform, predictable color than acid stain and is brighter — including blues, reds, and greens that acid stain cannot produce. Acid stain delivers variegated, mineral-toned color that reads as more natural and weathered. Dye runs $3-$8 per square foot installed vs $2-$6 for acid stain. Dye is the right answer when color predictability matters; acid stain is the right answer when an organic, one-of-a-kind look matters.

faqs.question: What is microtopping and how much does it cost?
faqs.answer: Microtopping is a 1/16 to 1/8 inch polymer-modified cement topping troweled over cured concrete to create a smooth, paintable surface. It's used to refresh worn slabs without full replacement. Cost: $4-$10 per square foot installed in 2026. Microtopping can be colored, dyed, or stamped before it cures. It's the closest analog to repainting a concrete floor and is a popular finish on basement floors and interior commercial concrete.

faqs.question: How much does a stamped overlay cost?
faqs.answer: Stamped overlay (3/8-inch polymer-modified concrete topping that's then stamped while still plastic) runs $7-$15 per square foot installed in 2026 — typically $9-$12 per square foot. On a 400-square-foot patio that's $3,600-$4,800. Stamped overlay is cheaper than tearing out and replacing with new stamped concrete ($10-$18/sf) but only works when the underlying slab is structurally sound.

faqs.question: Can I do decorative concrete myself?
faqs.answer: Acid stain and water-based dye are the most DIY-feasible decorative concrete methods at $0.40-$1.20 per square foot in material on a 400-square-foot slab. Acid stain requires safety equipment (acid-resistant gloves, eye protection, respirator). Stamped overlay and microtopping have trickier application windows and pattern alignment — DIY failure rate is meaningfully higher. According to industry data, professional acid-stain has roughly an 85 percent first-attempt success rate vs 50-60 percent for DIY.

faqs.question: How long does decorative concrete last?
faqs.answer: Acid stain and dye penetrate the concrete and last indefinitely as long as the sealer is maintained (reseal every 2-3 years interior, 1-2 years exterior). Microtopping lasts 10-20 years interior, 8-12 years exterior before requiring reapplication. Stamped overlay lasts 10-20 years before color and pattern need refresh. The underlying slab continues to age at its normal rate (25-50 years residential) regardless of decorative treatment.

faqs.question: Does decorative concrete crack?
faqs.answer: Yes — and any cracks in the underlying slab will telegraph through the decorative treatment. Acid stain and dye penetrate the slab and crack with it. Microtopping and stamped overlay bond to the substrate and follow cracks within 6-24 months. According to the American Concrete Institute, decorative treatments do not bridge structural cracks and should not be applied over slabs with active settlement or wide cracks until those are repaired.

faqs.question: What's the cheapest way to upgrade an ugly concrete slab?
faqs.answer: Concrete sealer with a tint ($1-$2.50 per square foot, DIY-friendly) is the cheapest upgrade — gives a uniform tone and protects the slab without full decorative treatment. Next cheapest: single-color water-based dye with sealer ($3-$5 per square foot installed). Beyond that, acid stain ($3-$4 per square foot typical) delivers the biggest visual transformation per dollar. Avoid floor paint, which fails within 2-3 years on exterior or vehicle-traffic concrete.

howToSteps.name: Surface preparation
howToSteps.text: Existing concrete is cleaned (degreased, pressure-washed, neutralized of any prior sealer). Cracks are repaired and the surface is scrubbed to a clean, lightly textured profile. This step is roughly 30-40 percent of total labor and is the most common cause of decorative-concrete failure when skipped.

howToSteps.name: Apply acid stain or dye
howToSteps.text: For acid stain, the etching solution is sprayed or brushed onto the surface in 1-2 coats and allowed to react 1-4 hours before neutralization. For water-based dye, the dye is sprayed in 1-3 coats with drying between coats. Multiple colors are typically applied in sequence to build the final visual.

howToSteps.name: Neutralize and rinse
howToSteps.text: Acid stain residue is neutralized with a baking soda or ammonia solution and rinsed with clean water until the rinse water comes off clear. This step is critical — residual acid breaks down sealer and causes blooming.

howToSteps.name: Cure and dry
howToSteps.text: The surface dries 24-48 hours before sealer application. Dry humid climates may extend this; areas with high humidity or rain delay the sealing step. Premature sealing traps moisture and causes sealer failure.

howToSteps.name: Apply sealer
howToSteps.text: Acrylic or water-based polyurethane sealer is applied in 1-2 coats by sprayer, roller, or brush. Interior sealers prioritize low VOC; exterior sealers prioritize UV resistance. Wax topcoats are sometimes applied over sealer for added scuff resistance on high-traffic floors.

howToSteps.name: Cure sealer
howToSteps.text: Foot traffic permitted at 24 hours; vehicle traffic at 48-72 hours; full sealer cure at 7 days. Stay off the surface during cure to avoid scuff marks. The first wash of the floor should wait at least 30 days to avoid loosening fresh sealer.

content:
Quick Answer: Decorative concrete costs $2-$15 per square foot in 2026 depending on method. Acid stain: $2-$6/sf. Water-based dye: $3-$8/sf. Polished concrete: $4-$8/sf. Microtopping overlay: $4-$10/sf. Stamped overlay: $7-$15/sf. Each method has a different visual, lifespan, and substrate requirement. Surface preparation is the single biggest determinant of whether the treatment lasts 15 years or fails in 18 months.

Decorative concrete is the most-confusing residential concrete category because the term covers six meaningfully different finish methods, each with its own cost, lifespan, and visual signature. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 pricing for the four most common methods applied to existing residential slabs — acid stain, water-based dye, microtopping, and stamped overlay — plus polished concrete and the sealer-only entry point.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed decorative-concrete, slab, driveway, and patio prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, decorative concrete pricing spans $2-$15 per square foot installed depending on method: acid stain $2-$6, water-based dye $3-$8, polished concrete $4-$8, microtopping overlay $4-$10, and stamped overlay $7-$15. Each method has a different appearance, durability profile, and substrate requirement — acid stain etches the surface to produce variegated mineral colors that no two slabs share, while dye produces uniform tone and overlay methods build a new surface on top of cured concrete. The four methods together cover the most common decorative-concrete jobs on existing residential slabs: refreshing a garage floor, transforming an unfinished basement, dressing up an exterior patio, or rescuing a worn-out driveway.

2026 decorative concrete pricing by method

National typical installed pricing for each decorative method applied to existing residential concrete that's been properly prepared (clean, no major repairs needed). Includes labor, material, sealer, and standard cleanup.

Method2026 cost per sfBest visual fitLifespanDIY-feasible?
Tinted sealer only$1-$2.50Uniform light tone refresh3-5 years (reseal)Yes
Acid stain$2-$6Variegated mineral tones, antiqueIndefinite (reseal)Yes with care
Water-based dye$3-$8Uniform predictable color, brightIndefinite (reseal)Yes
Polished concrete$4-$8Smooth, sealed, exposed aggregate20+ yearsNo (equipment heavy)
Microtopping overlay$4-$10Smooth refresh of worn slab10-20 yearsNo (timing critical)
Stamped overlay$7-$15Pattern + texture on existing slab10-20 yearsNo

Most homeowners deciding between methods land on acid stain or water-based dye for interior floors and basement applications, microtopping for worn commercial-look concrete, and stamped overlay for outdoor patios and pool decks where a true stamped finish wasn't done at the original pour.

Acid stain vs water-based dye

This is the most common decorative-concrete decision homeowners make. Both penetrate the concrete and stay forever (with sealer maintenance). The trade is visual: acid stain creates one-of-a-kind variegation; dye creates predictable uniform color.

Acid stain

Acid stain is a chemical etching agent — typically a metallic salt solution diluted in water and a small amount of muriatic acid — that reacts with the calcium hydroxide in cured concrete to produce permanent mineral color. The reaction produces variegated, mottled, weathered-looking color that varies from slab to slab and from spot to spot on the same slab.

  • Cost: $2-$6 per square foot installed in 2026 ($3-$4 typical)
  • Color palette: earth tones — browns, tans, terra cotta, blacks, greens, blue-grays. Bright primary colors not possible.
  • Best fit: interior floors with rustic, antique, or organic design intent; concrete that already has slight variation in surface texture.
  • Failure modes: uneven application = blotchy result; residual acid not neutralized = sealer failure; surface contamination = no color reaction.

According to the American Concrete Institute, acid-stain color depth and consistency depend heavily on the cement-paste content of the surface and the surface texture. New concrete (less than 60 days old) doesn't have enough crystalline calcium hydroxide to take the stain fully — staining is best done at 60+ days after pour.

Water-based dye

Water-based dye is a colorant that penetrates the concrete and produces uniform color without etching. The color palette is wide — including primary colors not possible with acid stain. Color is more predictable across the slab and from job to job.

  • Cost: $3-$8 per square foot installed in 2026 ($4-$5 typical)
  • Color palette: wide — including reds, blues, greens, yellows in addition to earth tones
  • Best fit: interior floors where uniform color matters; brand or design-driven color schemes; floors with multiple zones of distinct color
  • Failure modes: applied over inadequate surface = pooling and blotchy result; applied over sealed surface = no penetration; outdoor UV exposure fades some dye colors over 5-10 years

Overlay methods: microtopping and stamped overlay

Both methods build a new cementitious surface on top of cured concrete rather than coloring the existing surface. Use when the existing slab is structurally sound but surface-worn, contaminated, or just visually unsatisfactory.

Microtopping

A 1/16 to 1/8 inch polymer-modified cement topping troweled smooth over cured concrete. Can be colored, dyed, or stamped after application. Common in commercial retail and high-end residential interior floors where a clean smooth finish is the goal.

  • Cost: $4-$10 per square foot installed in 2026 ($6-$8 typical)
  • Lifespan: 10-20 years interior; 8-12 years exterior
  • Best fit: basement floors, commercial retail, interior residential where existing concrete is worn but structurally sound
  • Limitations: won't bridge cracks; cracks in underlying slab telegraph through within 12-24 months

Stamped overlay

A 3/8 inch polymer-modified concrete topping that's troweled over cured concrete and then stamped with pattern mats while plastic. Used to add stamped finish to existing concrete that wasn't originally stamped, or to refresh stamped concrete that's faded or worn.

  • Cost: $7-$15 per square foot installed in 2026 ($9-$12 typical)
  • Lifespan: 10-20 years before color and pattern need refresh
  • Best fit: patios, pool decks, driveways where adding stamped pattern to existing slab is desired
  • Vs new stamped concrete: overlay is 30-50 percent cheaper than tearing out and pouring new stamped, when the existing slab is structurally sound

For full stamped concrete pricing including new-pour vs overlay context, see Cost of Concrete's stamped concrete cost guide.

Polished concrete

Polished concrete is mechanically ground and polished with progressively finer diamond abrasives to expose the aggregate and create a smooth, sealed, durable surface. Common in commercial retail, warehouses, and high-end residential interior. It's a finish, not a coating — the surface is the slab itself.

  • Cost: $4-$8 per square foot installed in 2026 for typical residential ($6 typical)
  • Lifespan: 20+ years before requiring re-polish
  • Best fit: interior floors where durability and easy cleaning matter; minimalist design intent
  • Visual: matte to high-gloss depending on final grit; exposed aggregate produces unique speckled look
  • Limitations: not feasible exterior; slippery when wet; expensive specialty equipment makes DIY impractical

Per the Portland Cement Association, polished concrete has grown significantly as a commercial flooring option over the last decade because of its low total cost of ownership — no replacement coating cycle, easy cleaning, and 20+ year lifespan.

Surface prep: the make-or-break step

Surface preparation is roughly 30-40 percent of the total labor on any decorative concrete job and is the most common cause of failure. The required prep depends on the slab condition and the decorative method.

Slab conditionPrep requiredAdditional cost
Clean unsealed concreteSweep, vacuum, light degreaseIncluded in baseline
Lightly soiled or stainedPressure wash, degreaser+$0.50-$1.00/sf
Sealed concreteStrip existing sealer chemically or mechanically+$1.00-$3.00/sf
Heavily contaminatedMechanical grinding to clean substrate+$2.00-$5.00/sf
Cracked or settledRepair before decorative; cracks will telegraphVaries — see concrete repair cost guide

How decorative concrete is installed

  1. Surface preparation. Existing concrete is cleaned (degreased, pressure-washed, neutralized of any prior sealer). Cracks are repaired and the surface is scrubbed to a clean, lightly textured profile. This step is roughly 30-40 percent of total labor and is the most common cause of decorative-concrete failure when skipped.
  2. Apply acid stain or dye. For acid stain, the etching solution is sprayed or brushed onto the surface in 1-2 coats and allowed to react 1-4 hours before neutralization. For water-based dye, the dye is sprayed in 1-3 coats with drying between coats. Multiple colors are typically applied in sequence to build the final visual.
  3. Neutralize and rinse. Acid stain residue is neutralized with a baking soda or ammonia solution and rinsed with clean water until the rinse water comes off clear. This step is critical — residual acid breaks down sealer and causes blooming.
  4. Cure and dry. The surface dries 24-48 hours before sealer application. Dry humid climates may extend this; areas with high humidity or rain delay the sealing step. Premature sealing traps moisture and causes sealer failure.
  5. Apply sealer. Acrylic or water-based polyurethane sealer is applied in 1-2 coats by sprayer, roller, or brush. Interior sealers prioritize low VOC; exterior sealers prioritize UV resistance. Wax topcoats are sometimes applied over sealer for added scuff resistance on high-traffic floors.
  6. Cure sealer. Foot traffic permitted at 24 hours; vehicle traffic at 48-72 hours; full sealer cure at 7 days. Stay off the surface during cure to avoid scuff marks. The first wash of the floor should wait at least 30 days to avoid loosening fresh sealer.

For overlay methods (microtopping, stamped overlay), an additional substrate primer step is added between surface prep and topping application. Per the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, water-based primers and sealers are the residential standard because of low VOC content.

Sealing and maintenance

Every decorative concrete method requires sealer maintenance to hold its visual:

  • Interior acid stain / dye: reseal every 2-5 years depending on traffic
  • Exterior acid stain / dye: reseal every 1-2 years (UV breaks down sealer faster outdoors)
  • Microtopping interior: reseal every 2-3 years
  • Stamped overlay: reseal every 2-3 years
  • Polished concrete: burnish and reseal every 5-7 years

Reseal cost: $0.50-$1.50 per square foot. For a 400-square-foot patio, $200-$600 every reseal cycle. The single biggest determinant of whether decorative concrete looks like new at year 15 or looks faded at year 5 is sealer maintenance discipline.

Regional pricing variation

Decorative concrete pricing varies 25-45 percent between U.S. metros. Highest pricing: Northeast and West Coast at $5-$18 per square foot across methods. Lowest: South-Central U.S. (Texas, Oklahoma, Tennessee) where decorative concrete has been mainstream longest and skilled-crew supply is deepest, at $2-$10 per square foot. For full decorative-concrete service context, see Cost of Concrete's stamped and decorative concrete service hub.

Frequently asked questions

How much does acid stain cost on concrete in 2026?

Acid stain runs $2-$6 per square foot installed in 2026, with $3-$4 per square foot typical for a single-color application with sealer on a clean prepared slab. On a 400-square-foot patio, that's $800-$2,400 total — typically $1,200-$1,600. Acid stain etches the concrete surface to produce variegated mineral colors; no two slabs ever look identical.

Is concrete dye better than acid stain?

Concrete dye delivers more uniform, predictable color than acid stain and is brighter — including blues, reds, and greens that acid stain cannot produce. Acid stain delivers variegated, mineral-toned color that reads as more natural and weathered. Dye runs $3-$8 per square foot installed vs $2-$6 for acid stain. Dye is the right answer when color predictability matters; acid stain is the right answer when an organic, one-of-a-kind look matters.

What is microtopping and how much does it cost?

Microtopping is a 1/16 to 1/8 inch polymer-modified cement topping troweled over cured concrete to create a smooth, paintable surface. It's used to refresh worn slabs without full replacement. Cost: $4-$10 per square foot installed in 2026. Microtopping can be colored, dyed, or stamped before it cures. It's the closest analog to repainting a concrete floor and is a popular finish on basement floors and interior commercial concrete.

How much does a stamped overlay cost?

Stamped overlay (3/8-inch polymer-modified concrete topping that's then stamped while still plastic) runs $7-$15 per square foot installed in 2026 — typically $9-$12 per square foot. On a 400-square-foot patio that's $3,600-$4,800. Stamped overlay is cheaper than tearing out and replacing with new stamped concrete ($10-$18/sf) but only works when the underlying slab is structurally sound.

Can I do decorative concrete myself?

Acid stain and water-based dye are the most DIY-feasible decorative concrete methods at $0.40-$1.20 per square foot in material on a 400-square-foot slab. Acid stain requires safety equipment (acid-resistant gloves, eye protection, respirator). Stamped overlay and microtopping have trickier application windows and pattern alignment — DIY failure rate is meaningfully higher. According to industry data, professional acid-stain has roughly an 85 percent first-attempt success rate vs 50-60 percent for DIY.

How long does decorative concrete last?

Acid stain and dye penetrate the concrete and last indefinitely as long as the sealer is maintained (reseal every 2-3 years interior, 1-2 years exterior). Microtopping lasts 10-20 years interior, 8-12 years exterior before requiring reapplication. Stamped overlay lasts 10-20 years before color and pattern need refresh. The underlying slab continues to age at its normal rate (25-50 years residential) regardless of decorative treatment.

Yes — and any cracks in the underlying slab will telegraph through the decorative treatment. Acid stain and dye penetrate the slab and crack with it. Microtopping and stamped overlay bond to the substrate and follow cracks within 6-24 months. According to the American Concrete Institute, decorative treatments do not bridge structural cracks and should not be applied over slabs with active settlement or wide cracks until those are repaired.

What's the cheapest way to upgrade an ugly concrete slab?

Concrete sealer with a tint ($1-$2.50 per square foot, DIY-friendly) is the cheapest upgrade — gives a uniform tone and protects the slab without full decorative treatment. Next cheapest: single-color water-based dye with sealer ($3-$5 per square foot installed). Beyond that, acid stain ($3-$4 per square foot typical) delivers the biggest visual transformation per dollar. Avoid floor paint, which fails within 2-3 years on exterior or vehicle-traffic concrete.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 decorative concrete pricing: acid stain $2-$6/sf, dye $3-$8/sf, polished $4-$8/sf, microtopping $4-$10/sf, stamped overlay $7-$15/sf.
  • Acid stain produces one-of-a-kind variegated mineral color; dye produces uniform predictable color including bright primaries.
  • Microtopping and stamped overlay are surface-building methods — use when existing slab is structurally sound but visually unsatisfactory.
  • Surface prep is 30-40 percent of total labor and the most common point of failure when skipped.
  • Reseal every 2-3 years interior, 1-2 years exterior — sealer maintenance discipline is what separates 15-year results from 5-year failures.
  • Cracks in underlying slab telegraph through any decorative treatment; repair before applying.
  • Cheapest entry point: tinted sealer at $1-$2.50/sf; cheapest decorative method with real visual: acid stain at $3-$4/sf typical.

Need a contractor whose decorative concrete quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing.
